coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence and photocatalytic activity of metal-free nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with varying nitrogen contents
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yingying
Guan, Rentian
Zhang, Cong
Zhang, Keying
Liu, Wenjing
Shao, Xiaodong
Xue, Qingwang
Yue, Qiaoli
coredata.dc:description: A metal-free material was suggested for the photocatalysis of organic dye degradation with a higher catalysis rate based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) than those for most of previous works. A simple one-pot solvothermal strategy was employed to prepare nitrogen-doped (N-doped) CQDs using different precursors with varying nitrogen contents. As the carbon and nitrogen sources, aniline, 2-aminonaphthalene, 2-anthracylamine, and 1-aminopyrene were used as precursors for the synthesis of four CQDs (abbreviation: CD1, CD2, CD3, CD4), respectively. The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs all exceeded 0.10, and even arrived at 0.29 for CD3. In addition, the structural, morphological and optical properties of CQDs were characterized by multiple analytical techniques such as X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. CQDs show excellent photocatalytic activity. Four organic dyes including indigo carmine (IC), ethyl violet (EV), congo red (CR), and bordeaux R (Bo-R) were used as models to study the photocatalytic activity of CQDs. Importantly, the degradation efficiency reaches 97% for IC using CD2 under natural light 2 h and the degradation rate constant is 0.0309 min−1. The results suggest that the present CQDs products have potential applications in the degradation of organic dyes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Different precursors
Photocatalytic activity
Degradation rate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of reaction time and temperature
Effect of pH and buffer species

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and the size distribution graphics of CD1 (A), CD2 (B), CD3 (C) and CD4 (D), respectively.
XRD spectra of CD1 (A), CD2 (B), CD3 (C) and CD4 (D), respectively.
FTIR spectra of CD1 (A), CQDs and raw materials spectra of CD2 (B), CD3 (C) and CD4 (D), respectively.
UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of CD1 (A), CD2 (B), CD3 (C) and CD4 (D), the photograph of four CQDs solid and CQDs solution under room light and UV light illuminations (E), respectively.
UV–vis spectra of IC (A), EV (B), CR (C) and Bo-R (D) before and after the addition of CD1, CD2, CD3 and CD4 under natural light for 2 h, respectively.
Time dependent UV–vis spectra of IC with CD2 (A), the corresponding plot of ln (Ct/C0) vs. time of IC with CD2 (B), and digital images of IC solutions under different degradation times with CD2 (C).
Cycling runs for the IC photocatalytic degradation over CD2.
Schematic illustration for the catalytic degradation mechanism of CQDs on organic dyes.
Degradation of IC over CD2 with or without the scavenger.
Synthesis of CQDs and the degradation process of the organic dyes.
Results of dye degradation efficiency with different material.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence and photocatalytic activity of metal-free nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots with varying nitrogen contents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng 252059, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng 252059, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Performance and Structure Safety of Petroleum Tubular Goods and Equipment Materials, Tubular Goods Research Institute, Xian 710077, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Performance and Structure Safety of Petroleum Tubular Goods and Equipment Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Performance and Structure Safety of Petroleum Tubular Goods and Equipment Materials, Tubular Goods Research Institute, Xian 710077, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A metal-free material was suggested for the photocatalysis of organic dye degradation with a higher catalysis rate based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) than those for most of previous works. A simple one-pot solvothermal strategy was employed to prepare nitrogen-doped (N-doped) CQDs using different precursors with varying nitrogen contents. As the carbon and nitrogen sources, aniline, 2-aminonaphthalene, 2-anthracylamine, and 1-aminopyrene were used as precursors for the synthesis of four CQDs (abbreviation: CD1, CD2, CD3, CD4), respectively. The fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs all exceeded 0.10, and even arrived at 0.29 for CD3. In addition, the structural, morphological and optical properties of CQDs were characterized by multiple analytical techniques such as X-ray diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. CQDs show excellent photocatalytic activity. Four organic dyes including indigo carmine (IC), ethyl violet (EV), congo red (CR), and bordeaux R (Bo-R) were used as models to study the photocatalytic activity of CQDs. Importantly, the degradation efficiency reaches 97% for IC using CD2 under natural light 2 h and the degradation rate constant is 0.0309 min−1. The results suggest that the present CQDs products have potential applications in the degradation of organic dyes.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted great concern due to their unique properties since their discovery in 2004 [1]. CQDs possess highly abundant hydrophilic functional groups in their structures, such as carboxyl, amino and so on. Moreover, CQDs have high solubility, robust chemical inertness, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, low cost and facile modification [2,3]. Therefore, CQDs are considered as a kind of promising nanomaterial for a wide range of applications in biomedical areas including bioimaging [4], drug delivery and gene therapy [5], nanomedical [6], and non-biomedical areas such as photocatalysis [7], and sensors [8].

For the preparation of CQDs, they can be generated using top-down or bottom-up methods. By comparison with top-down route, the bottom-up process is facile and environmentally friendly. Thousands of hundred precursor materials are available to obtain CQDs, including natural and chemical synthetic substances. There are mature greatly methods for the preparation of CQDs, such as electrochemical oxidation [7], solvothermal/hydrothermal treatment [9,10], microwave assisted synthesis [11] and UV irradiation method [12] etc. Importantly, the hydrothermal method is widely used owing to the simple process, easy control and environmentally friendly products. The photoluminescence properties of CQDs are mostly attributed to their core structures and surface states [13]. So, doping with heteroatoms (e.g., oxygen, nitrogen, phosphor, boron or sulfur) is an effective method to tune their optical properties. Moreover, the quantum yield (QY) of doping CQDs is higher than free-doping CQDs. Sun et al. prepared highly luminescent N-doped CQDs and Ding et al. synthesis of N, S-doped CQDs with high QY [14,15]. Therefore, the QY of CQDs can be enhanced by doping heteroatomic element to change the surface of CQDs.

With the rapid development of industry, environmental pollution is a major issue that has attracted the attention of the public and researchers. Various types of pollutants discharged from chemical plants enter into water bodies and cause environmental pollution [16]. However, organic dye, as a persistent pollutant, plays a key role in environmental pollution. Organic dyes are widely used in ink [17], textile [18], paper [19], leather [20] and so on. At present, the production of organic dyes accounts for about a quarter of the total output of dyes. With the widespread use of organic dyes, the degradation of organic dyes has attracted the attention of researchers and environmental scientists, which is a huge challenge task for them. So far, many treatment technologies, for instance, adsorption [21], photocatalysis [22], biodegradation [23] etc., have been applied in dyes degradation. Among these technologies, photocatalysis is a vital method for dyes degradation due to its ability to change the organic compounds into inorganic compounds, displaying enormous practical and potential values. Therefore, it is important to develop and utilize efficient catalysis for dyes degradation in wastewater.

In the past decades, semiconductor photocatalyst is considered as one of the most efficient means to mitigate organic dyes in water. However, low activity under visible light and photogenerated electron and hole pairs sharp recombination reduced its photocatalytic performance and limited its large-scale application [24]. In order to solve this problem, researchers have proposed proper modification or hybridization to improve their photocatalytic performance. CQDs, due to its unique photocatalytic properties, such as high absorption coefficients and fast electron transport, can be used to achieve enhanced purposes of improving photocatalytic performance by modification or hybridization between CQDs and semiconductors. Based on the idea, lots of efficient catalysts based on CQDs have been achieved and used degradation of organic dyes [25,26]. Therefore, it can be seen that CQDs-based photocatalysts have been used widely. However, there are a few reports about metal-free CQDs used as catalysts to remove pollutants or degrade organic dyes. Cheng et al. [27] have reported CQDs used as photocatalysts to degrade organic dyes. Firstly, AMP was used to prepare CQDs, then surface modification of CQDs with ammonia, and thionyl chloride to obtained N-doped and Cl-doped CQDs for degradation of organic dyes. The degradation of N-doped CQDs on rhodamine B (RhB) is about 97% and the degradation rate constant is 0.02463 min−1 for 4 h irradiation. However, the degradation of Cl-doped CQDs on methylene blue (MB) and indigo carmine (IC) were 56% and 60% for the 4 h irradiation, respectively. The high catalytic efficiency of CQDs may be attributed to the variety of functional groups on the surface and show different catalytic performance [28,29]. In addition, the utilization efficiency of visible light can be improved by the modification of metals on the surface of CQDs [24]. For the metal-free photocatalysts, especially for sole CQDs, it is necessary to explore their potential application as the photocatalysis for the degradation of organic dyes.

In this work, we proposed a simple and environmentally friendly method to synthesize N-doped CQDs from four precursors with similar structures and their applications in efficient degradation of organic dyes. As Scheme 1 illustrates, N-doped CQDs synthesized via a one-pot solvothermal route, were ascribed from aniline, 2-aminonaphthalene, 2-anthracylamine, 1-aminopyrene, respectively. The N-doped CQDs are used to degrade IC, ethyl violet (EV), congo red (CR) and bordeaux R (Bo-R). The degradation process and schematic diagram are shown in Scheme 1.

CQDs were synthesized using a Teflon-sealed autoclave reactor in a DZ-2A vacuum drying oven (Tianjin Taisite Instrument Co., Ltd., http://www.taisite.cn) and pretreated in a KQ5200 Ultrasonic Cleaner (Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd., http://shop1304136.yellowurl.cn). Fluorescence spectra and intensity were obtained on a LS-55 spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA, http://yeepart.com). Absorption spectral measurements were carried out on a Lambda 750 spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were performed on a JEM 2100 electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan, https://www.jeol.co.jp). TEM worked with an acceleration voltage at 200 kV, and a carbon-coated copper grid was used for sample suspension. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were carried out on a Nicolet 6700 (Thermo Scientific Ltd., USA, http://www.thermofisher.com). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were undertaken with a K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Ltd., USA, http://www.thermofisher.com). The pHs-3C digital pH meter (Shanghai Leici Device Works, China) was used for the pH test with a combined glass-calomel electrode. Digital photos of CQDs dispersion in day light were captured by a Coolpix 4500 digital camera (Nikon, Japan, https://www.nikon.co.jp).

Aniline, 2-aminonaphthalene, 2-anthracylamine, 1-aminopyrene, indigo carmine, ethyl violet, congo red, bordeaux R were purchased from Shanghai PERFEMIKER Material Tech Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China, http://www.canspecsci.com). Acid, base and some common salts were ordered from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China, http://www.aladdin-e.com). There were HCl, HAc, NaOH, NaCl, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, NaAc, KI, NaC2O4. Tris (hydroxy-methyl)-aminomethane (Tris) and 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazine-1-ethane-sulfonic acid (HEPES), benzoquinoneand and quinine sulphate were obtained from Fluka (Buchs, Switzerland, http://www.fluka.org/fluka.php). All chemicals from commercial sources were of analytical grade and used directly without further purification if there was no special instructions. All solutions were prepared and diluted by ultrapure water from Milli-Q water purified system.

The synthesis of CQDs was carried out using hydrothermal methods by reference to previous work with some changes. Aniline (3.1 μL), 2-aminonaphthalene (4.8 mg), 2-anthracylamine (6.4 mg) and 1-aminopyrene (7.2 mg) was dissolved in 5 mL mixed solvent of potassium persulfate (0.01 M) and ethanol in the ratio of 3:2 and sonicated for 2 min, respectively. Then, they were transferred to autoclave reactor and heated at 180 °C for 10 h prepared N-doped CQDs, respectively. Then, the CQDs were centrifuged at 10, 000 rpm for 5 min to take the supernatant and small part supernatant was pretreated for 24 h using dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 3500 Da. Finally, CQDs suspension was obtained and stored at 4 °C for subsequent processes.

The respective individual dye solutions, IC, EV, CR and Bo-R were tested. 150 μL 0.2 mg/mL IC, EV, CR, Bo-R was mixed with 2.5 mL deionized water, respectively. Then, 350 μL as-prepared CQDs were added into the mixed solution and the mixed solution was continuously stirred for 10 min to achieve an adsorption–desorption equilibrium between dye and photocatalyst under without direct natural light. Finally, the catalytic degradation was carried out under natural light for 2 h. Furthermore, 350 μL deionized water instead of CQDs was added into the mixed solution for compared under the same conditions. UV–vis absorption spectrum was recorded at the absorption maximum wavelength of the dyes, respectively.

The QY of CQDs was calculated according to an established procedure. Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY of 54%) was chosen as a standard. The emission spectra at the excitation wavelength of 314, 365, 405, and 339 nm were recorded by fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the absorbance at excitation wavelength was measured by an UV–vis spectrophotometer, respectively. In addition, the absorbance value was acquired with the intensity below 0.05. All of the above measured integrated areas for the fluorescence emission peaks were obtained by the origin software. The QY of CQDs was determined at the excitation wavelength by the following equation:
(1)
Q X
=
Q ST
m X
/
m ST
η 2
X
/
η 2
ST
where Q is the QY, m is the slope (the gradient from the plot of integrated emission fluorescence intensity vs absorbance), η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “ST” refers to standard with known QY and “X” for the sample.

The first-principles calculations in this study were carried out based on a pseudo potential plane-wave method using VASP package [30]. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with the functional of Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) [31] was employed to describe the electron exchange-correlation interactions. The cutoff of plane-wave kinetic energy was set to be 400 eV, and the k-point sampling employs a 2 × 2 × 1 mesh within the Monkhorst–Pack scheme. A vacuum layer of 20 Å was built to prevent interactions between the two repeated layers.

CD1, CD2, CD3, CD4 were synthesized by a facile, one-pot solvothermal route using four different precursors, respectively. Fig. 1 show the representative TEM images of CD1, CD2, CD3, CD4, which are in monodispersion without obvious aggregation. Size histograms (inset of Fig. 1) of CD1, CD2, CD3, CD4 were obtained by the statistical analysis of TEM images, and particle diameters at 2.0 ± 0.2 nm, 2.2 ± 0.2 nm, 2.6 ± 0.2 nm and 2.2 ± 0.2 nm, respectively.

XRD patterns of CD1, CD2, CD3, CD4 are shown in Fig. 2 . XRD patterns of four CQDs display a broad peak around 26°, respectively. It refers to the carbon phase (002), corresponding to a graphite structure.

FT-IR spectra of CD1, CD2, CD3, CD4 and precursor material are shown in Fig. 3 . For four CQDs, there are a broad peak at 3000–3500 cm−1 and some peaks at 3417, 3260, 3418, 3428 cm−1 respectively, which are attributed to the O–H and N–H stretching vibrations. The peaks located at 1726, 1644, 1720, 1722, and 1633 cm−1 belong to CO stretching absorption and the band at 1427, 1428, and 1434 cm−1 can be assigned to the bending vibrations of N–H group [10]. IR bands within the range of 1000–1400 cm−1, which indicate the stretching vibrations of C-N, C-O and C-S functional groups presence in the CQDs (the peaks of CD1, CD2, CD3, CD4 at 1200, 1057, 1012 cm−1; 1239, 1071, 1014 cm−1; 1215, 1063 cm−1; 1224, 1064, 1008 cm−1, respectively.) [15]. The absorption bands at 1000–665 cm−1 are assigned to benzene ring bending vibration of C–H (the peaks of CD1, CD2, CD3, CD4 at 917, 839, 771 cm−1; 921, 855, 788 cm−1; 852 cm−1; 925, 850, 787 cm−1, respectively.) It can be seen from the observation results that CQDs are mainly composed of C, O, N and S.

To further investigate the element compositions and surface states of CD1, CD2, CD3 and CD4, the X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were carried out (Fig. S1-4, in Supporting Information). Fig. S1A (in Supporting Information) displays four typical peaks at 168.3 eV, 284.4 eV, 400.2 eV and 531.9 eV, which are corresponding to S2p, C1s, N1s, O1s, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1B (in Supporting Information), the O1s high-resolution spectrum of CD1 shows characteristic peak at 531.6 eV. As illustrated in Fig. S1C (in Supporting Information), the N1s spectrum exhibits five peaks at 398.6 eV, 399.2 eV, 400.1 eV, 401.0 eV and 401.9 eV, which are associated with pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, C-N and graphitic-N, respectively. As exhibited in Fig. S1D (in Supporting Information), the C1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into four peaks centered at 284.2 eV, 284.7 eV, 285.9 eV and 288.3 eV, which are assigned to C–C/CC/C–H (284.2 eV and 284.7 eV), C-S/C-O/C-N and CO/CN, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1E (in Supporting Information), the S2p spectrum displays two peaks at 168.0 eV and 168.9 eV, which are assigned to the oxidized sulfur groups, i.e., -C- S(O)x-C- bonds (x = 2, 3, 4) rather from the -C-S- bond [15,32]. Fig. S2A (in Supporting Information) illustrates four dominant peaks at 168.3 eV, 284.4 eV, 400.2 eV and 532.0 eV of S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s for CD2, respectively. As shown in Fig. S2B, C, D, E (in Supporting Information), the O1s high-resolution spectrum of CD2 shows characteristic peak at 531.7 eV, which is similar to CD1. The N1s spectrum shows four peaks at 398.7 eV, 399.4 eV, 399.9 and 401.7 eV, which are associated with pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N, respectively. The C1s spectrum displays four peaks at 284.1 eV, 284.6 eV, 286.0 and 288.4 eV and the S2p spectrum illustrates two peaks at 168.1 eV and 169.0 eV are similar with CD1. Fig. S3A (in Supporting Information) exhibits four typical peaks at 168.4 eV, 284.4 eV, 400.1 eV and 532.0 eV, which are attributed to S2p, C1s, N1s, O1s, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3B, C, D, E (in Supporting Information), the O1s high-resolution spectrum of CD3 shows characteristic peak at 531.9 eV, which is similar to CD1. The N1s spectrum exhibits four peaks at 399.0 eV, 399.8 eV, 400.8 eV and 402.0 eV, which are associated with pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, C-N and graphitic-N, respectively. The C1s spectrum demonstrates four peaks at 284.0 eV, 284.6 eV, 285.9 eV and 288.3 eV and the S2p spectrum displays two peaks at 168.2 eV and 169.2 eV, which are similar with CD1. Fig. S4A (in Supporting Information) shows four main peaks at 168.2 eV, 284.8 eV, 399.9 eV and 532.2 eV, which belong to S2p, C1s, N1s, O1s, respectively. As shown in Fig. S4B, C, D, E (in Supporting Information), the O1s high-resolution spectrum of CD4 shows characteristic peak at 532.0 eV, which is similar to CD3. The N1s spectrum exhibits three peaks at 399.2 eV, 399.7 eV and 401.8 eV, which are associated with pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N, respectively. The C1s spectrum demonstrates four peaks at 284.2 eV, 284.6 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.4 eV and the S2p spectrum displays two peaks at 168.2 eV and 169.1 eV, which are similar with CD1. The elemental content values of CD1, CD2, CD3 and CD4 are shown in Fig. S1-4 (in Supporting Information). It reveals that the relative atomic percentage value of O, C, N, S, respectively. As can be seen from the results, the percentage of four elements in CD1, CD2, CD3 and CD4 are similar. Moreover, it can be seen that their nitrogen contents are different, which are 4.01%, 2.48%, 5.29%, 2.65% for CD1, CD2, CD3 and CD4, respectively. In addition, it can also be seen that the XPS peaks position at high-resolution image of the four CQDs are also similar, which may be attributed to their precursor with similar structures. However, the peak intensity is completely different, which may be due to the different bonding degree of each chemical bond in CQDs.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained to study the optical properties of CQDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , it displays UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra and photograph of four CQDs solid and solution under room light and UV light illuminations. From the spectra, it can be seen the fluorescence spectra of four CQDs are in the visible region. Fig. 4A shows two clear absorption bands at 236 and 306 nm, the two peaks at 236 and 306 nm are typically ascribed to π-π* transition of CC bond in aromatic sp2 domainsand and n-π* transition of CO bond [33]. Fig. 4B, the CD2 absorption peaks are observed at 226 and 283 nm. The two absorption shoulders can be assigned to the π-π* transition of conjugated skeletons and the n-π* transition of heteroatom-containing bonds [34], respectively. Fig. 4C, the CD3 exhibits three UV absorption peaks around at 256, 318 and 410 nm. The two peaks at 256 and 318 nm, assigned to the n-π* transition of C-O and CO or C-N functional groups present at edges of CQDs [35]. In addition, a distinct absorption band from 385 to 440 nm is also observed in the lower-energy region, such absorption characteristic is usually considered to originate from the defect states caused by the functional groups on the CQDs surfaces [36]. Fig. 4D, the CD4 shows absorption peaks at 242 and 274 nm, attributable to the π-π* of CC bond. In addition, a abroad peak between 350 and 500 nm assigns for n-π* transitions of CO, CN groups [9]. For four CQDs, the excitation/emission peak are at 314/406 nm, 365/445 nm, 405/510 nm and 339/440 nm, respectively. Fig. 4E, the photograph of four CQDs solid and solution samples are shown. From the photographs, the four CQDs solution show different colors under room light. The solution of CD1, CD2, CD4 show bright blue fluorescence and CD3 shows bright yellow-green fluorescence under the UV light. However, the four CQDs solid samples show almost the same color under room light and they all show bright blue fluorescence under the UV light.

To obtain the optimal excitation wavelength for CD1, CD2, CD3 and CD4, the fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths were recorded (Fig. S5, in Supporting Information). It can be observed that the peak intensity varies. Fig. S5A (in Supporting Information), as the excitation wavelength increases from 274 nm to 344 nm, the peak fluorescence intensity is lower than the optimal excitation wavelength and fluctuates up and down. Fig. S5B (in Supporting Information), along with the increase of excitation wavelength from 325 nm to 405 nm, the peak fluorescence intensity goes up and down with the maximum excitation wavelength at 365 nm while peak wavelength changes slightly. Fig. S5C (in Supporting Information), along with the increase of excitation wavelength from 365 nm to 445 nm, the peak fluorescence intensity goes up and down with the maximum excitation wavelength at 405 nm. Fig. S5D (in Supporting Information), the maximum excitation wavelength is at 339 nm.

As illustrated in Fig. S6 (in Supporting Information), quinine sulfate as a reference, the QY values of CD1, CD2, CD3 and CD4 were calculated as 0.25, 0.11, 0.28 and 0.14, respectively. It can be observed that under the same experimental conditions, the QY of CQDs does not increase with the increase of benzene ring. However, N-doped and S-doped can improve the QY of CQDs, while the higher doped content of S and N, the higher QY of CQDs (element content of four CQDs can be seen from Fig. S1-4A, in Supporting Information).
To achieve the optimum performance, the conditions for synthesis of CQDs were studied including reaction time and temperature, the impact of buffer species, pH and ion strength for as-prepared CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction time and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis scheme of CQDs is outlined in Scheme 1. The products CD1, CD2, CD3 and CD4 were obtained through a solvothermal method by treating aniline, 2-aminonaphthalene, 2-anthracylamine, 1-aminopyrene, respectively. The synthesis conditions were investigated by comparison of the optical property of CQDs, such as reaction time and solvothermal temperature. Fig. S7A, C, E and G (in Supporting Information) show that the heating time for solvothermal reaction display maximum value at 10 h for CQDs when changes from 8 to 12 h, respectively. Fig. S7B, D, F and H (in Supporting Information) show that the heating temperature for solvothermal reaction display maximum value at 180℃ when increase from 160℃ to 200℃, respectively. So, four CQDs products were prepared at 180 °C for 10 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and buffer species
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S8A (in Supporting Information), the fluorescence intensity increases first and then decreases for CD1 with pH values changes ranging from 2.0 to 6.0 and basically unchanged in weak acid and alkaline environments. As shown in Fig. S8B (in Supporting Information), under acidic and weak base conditions, ie pH range from 2.0 to 8.0, the CD2 fluorescence intensity first increases and then decreases, whereas from 8.0 to 12.0, not significantly change. As shown in Fig. S8C (in Supporting Information), the CD3 fluorescence does not change significantly in strong acid and alkali. There is a steady platform for the fluorescence intensity of CD3 in the range of 2.0–6.0 and 8.0–12.0. However, fluorescence intensity decreases linearly in the range 6.0–8.0. As shown in Fig. S8D (in Supporting Information), with pH changes from 2.0 to 7.0, the CD4 fluorescence intensity drops first then increases and decreases, but in the range of 8.0–12.0, its fluorescence intensity is basically unchanged. As for the effect of buffer composites on four CQDs, pure water, acetic acid (HAc), 2-[4-(2-Hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazinyl]ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), phosphate buffer (PB), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and Tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane hydrochloride buffer (Tris-HCl) were tested (Fig. S9). The fluorescence intensity of CQDs is different with varying buffer species and in any buffer is lower than in pure water. Therefore, the buffer solution affects the fluorescence intensity of CQDs.

Since salt tolerance is one of the most common property on CQDs, so the effect of different concentrations of NaCl was carried out. From Fig. S10A (in Supporting Information), it can be observed that the influence of NaCl concentration on the fluorescence of CD1 is tremendous and a sharp stepped down in the salt concentration range of 0–50 mM. Fig. S10B, C, D (in Supporting Information), it is demonstrated that the influence of NaCl concentration on the fluorescence of CD2, CD3 and CD4 is slight in the salt concentration range of 0–50 mM. It can be seen that the CD1 products not own outstanding ability of resisting salt and good stability while other three CQDs products possess.

In recent years, environmental water pollution has become increasingly serious, and the discharge of organic dyes is one of the crucial factors. To solve this problem, the photocatalytic activity of CQDs was studied. In this work, indigo carmine (IC), ethyl violet (EV), congo red (CR) and bordeaux R (Bo-R) were used as pollutant to evaluate photocatalytic performance of CQDs under natural light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 5 , UV–vis spectra of four dyes without-adding and adding CQDs were recorded. Four dyes can be degraded by four CQDs catalysts, but the efficiency of degradation is different. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency is calculated according to the following equation:
(2)
D =
C 0
-
C t
/
C 0
× 100 %
where C0 is the initial concentration of the dyes, and Ct is the concentrations of the dyes after degradation for t min, As shown in Fig. 5A, it can be seen that UV–vis spectra absorption peak intensity of IC solution in 611 nm decreases significantly adding CD2 and CD3, indicating that IC is degraded effectively by CD2 and CD3. According to Eq. (2), the calculated degradation efficiency of CD2 and CD3 on IC is about 97% and 93%, respectively. The degradation efficiency on IC by CD1 and CD4 is lower than CD2 and CD3, and degradation efficiency on IC of CD1 and CD4 is 52% and 26%, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5B, the absorption peak of EV solution in 596 nm after adding four CQDs is slightly weakened and the absorption peak of four CQDs on EV is similar. As shown in Fig. 5C and D, the absorption peak of CR and Bo-R solution in 499 nm and 524 nm cut down significantly adding CD1. It is indicated that CD1 has good degradation effect on CR and Bo-R. However, the peak intensity of CR and Bo-R after adding other three CQDs is slightly decline. Moreover, it can be seen that the degradation efficiency of three CQDs on CR is higher than on Bo-R. Through the above analysis, the degradation of CD2 on IC should be further studied.

For comparison, the degradation efficiency of the dye with different materials are listed in Table 1 . By comparison with previous works, the degradation efficiency of the present CQDs products is higher than those of the CQDs-metal complex materials. Furthermore, the carbon-based materials from metal-free precursors showed highest efficiency for organic dye degradation among the products listed here. Therefore, as one of the metal-free nanomaterials, CQDs have potential application in organic dye degradation. More importantly, for the metal-free CQDs the degradation rate of present products (0.0309 min−1) is larger than that of previous work (0.02463 min−1 [27]). In addition, In addition, CQDs were synthesized using a simple one-pot solvothermal method, while more complex steps were employed including synthesis and modification of nitrogen element on the surface of CQDs in the previous literature.

In order to explore optimum experimental conditions for catalytic degradation of IC, the effects of pH, CQDs dosage, dye concentration and light on degradation of IC were explored. As shown in Fig. S11A (in Supporting Information), with the increase of pH, not only the degradation rate increase of IC, but also leading increase in the degradation efficiency of IC. The removal efficiency of 47%, 97% and 99% for IC was observed at pH 2.06, 6.52 and 12.03, respectively. It shows that the greater pH value, the more reactive oxygen species (ROS) substance are generated, resulting in a faster degradation rate and a higher remove efficiency of IC. Fig. S11B (in Supporting Information) shows the effect of catalyst CQDs concentration on the degradation of IC. It shows that the degradation of IC was significantly influenced by the amount of catalyst. The remove efficiency enhanced when the catalyst CQDs dosage from 0.12 to 0.28 mg/mL. However, the degradation efficiency decreases of IC when the catalyst CQDs dose increased from 0.28 to 0.44 mg/mL. The reason may be ascribed to the fact that increasing the CQDs dose, which can be cluster in solution lead to reduce adsorption and desorption of IC. As shown in Fig. S11C (in Supporting Information), the effect of IC concentration (initial IC concentration of 0.01, 0.02, and 0.03 mg/mL) was monitored. The UV–vis absorbance spectra of IC supernatants after the photocatalytic tentative was recorded. It can be seen that IC concentration of 0.01–0.02 mg/mL, the dye is absolutely degradation. However, when the dye concentration is 0.02–0.03 mg/mL, the dye is almost completely degraded, but the degradation rate is not as high as the concentration is 0.01–0.02 mg/mL. In addition, the influence of light intensity on the degradation of dyes was also studied. Fig. S11D (in Supporting Information) shows the degradation efficiency under natural light is about one third of that under natural light. Its confirmed that IC was photocatalytic degraded and light intensity has a certain effect on the efficiency of catalytic degradation. However, due to the degradation efficiency of the dye is almost the same as that of the neutral and alkaline, and considering the influence of pH on the environment, the degradation of the dye will be performed under almost neutral conditions.

Photocatalysis is induced by produced carriers, such as excess electrons (e−) and holes (h+). When the photons with the energy of hv radiate CQDs, they are aroused by the photon, whose energy band is higher than CQDs, lead to the electrons from ground state transit to excited state [27] (as shown in Eq. (3)). Moreover, the generated electrons and holes are separated and moved to the surface of CQDs, and this process can promote the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as, O2·− and ·OH (as shown in Eq. (4, 5)). The photocatalytic degradation mechanism of CQDs on dyes is shown in Fig. 8 .
(3) CQDs + hv → e−+h+
(4) e−(CQDs) + O2 → O2 −
(5) h++H2O/OH–→OH

To further confirm the influences of the involved active species in the degradation of IC by CD2, several scavengers were used to remove the relevant active species involved in the degradation of dyes. For comparison, control experiment was performed without scavenger under the same conditions. The IC degradation efficiencies by the CD2 with different scavengers are shown in Fig. 9 . There is a greater conversion in an inert atmosphere after certain time, which confirms the role of O2 is less important. In addition, some reactive oxygen species (ROS) are known to affect the photocatalytic activity. Benzoquinone (BQ), potassium iodide (KI), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) and isopropanol (IPA) were used to scavenge O2·−, (h + and ·OH), h+ and ·OH, respectively [45,46]. As shown in Fig. 9, it can be seen that BQ has little effect on the IC degradation, indicating that O2·− play unimportant role in the degradation system. In addition, the degradation efficiency is similar after the addition of Na2C2O4 and IPA. However, after the addition of KI the degradation efficiency decreased obviously, which indicate that h+ and ·OH reactive species play a vital role and h+ and ·OH possess the major effect in removal of dyes with great contributions from other ROS. Therefore, h+ and ·OH play significant role in the degradation of IC.

To visualize the interaction between organic dye molecule and CQDs, the charge density difference was calculated using IC as a model dye with the results shown in Fig. S12 (in Supporting Information). The results show that as IC is adsorbed on the outer surface of CQDs, there is a charge transfer from IC to CQDs. The significant charge transfers between IC and CQDs demonstrate the strong ionic bond between them. The possible functional groups including –COOH, –SH, and –OH exhibit higher reaction activities than other groups on the surface of CQDs.

In summary, a simple approach is proposed to prepare N-doped CQDs products from different precursors (aniline, 2-aminonaphthalene, 2-anthracylamine, and 1-aminopyrene) by solvothermal method. Four CQDs all exhibit good fluorescence performance with QY exceeding 0.1 and the QY of CQDs from 2-aminoanthracene is even at 0.29. Four as-prepared CQDs have the uniform size and particle size about 2.0, 2.2, 2.6, 2.2 nm, respectively. Four CQDs show good photocatalytic performance. In addition, CD2 shows a high photocatalytic performance in degrading IC under natural light, and the degradation efficiency is about 97% for 2 h irradiation. The photocatalytic degradation of IC by CD2 is well fitted with pseudo-first-order kinetics and the degradation rate constant is 0.0309 min−1. The degradation efficiency values of IC for other CQDs are 26% (CD1), 93% (CD3) and 52% (CD4) under the natural light 2 h, respectively. Therefore, the results show that these CQDs have potential applications in the degradation of organic dyes.

Yingying Hu: Writing - original draft, Investigation. Rentian Guan: Partial auxiliary experiment. Cong Zhang: Partial auxiliary experiment. Keying Zhang: Partial auxiliary experiment. Wenjing Liu: Partial auxiliary experiment. Xiaodong Shao: Funding acquisition. Qingwang Xue: Funding acquisition. Qiaoli Yue: Project administration, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (91543206), the Natural Science Foundation (ZR2014BQ017, ZR2015BM024, and 2013SJGZ07), the Young Innovative Talents Introduction & Cultivation Program for Colleges and Universities of Shandong Province: Innovative Research Team on biomedical sensing and food safety Research and research foundation of Liaocheng University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2020.147344.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
